ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general glutinous rice dumpling class; no specific vegetarian zongzi subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific zongzi or vegetarian rice dumpling code. A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for vegetarian rice dumpling. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical vegetarian rice dumpling weighs approximately 180 g and constitutes a single serving as a snack or light meal.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Rice Dumpling — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetarian Rice Dumpling (CR ID: F07030013). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glutinous rice (sticky rice) has a published GI of approximately 98 per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. The vegetarian rice dumpling is composed predominantly of glutinous rice (~70% by weight), so GI 98 is applied as the primary driver. GL per serving calculated as: GI (98) × carbohydrate per serving (35.0 g/100g × 180 g serving = 63.0 g) / 100 = 61.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 455 mg per 100 g, exceeding the 400 mg/100 g threshold; driven by soy sauce in the filling with insufficient potassium or fibre offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit intake to half a dumpling per occasion, avoid additional soy sauce dipping, and balance the meal with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glutinous rice GI ~98 (very high) with 35 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 2.3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, producing a very high glycaemic load per serving (~61.7).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to a quarter to half dumpling per meal, pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and monitor blood glucose closely after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (455 mg/100 g) raises blood pressure risk; fat content is moderate at 4.1 g/100 g; saturated fat breakdown is unavailable but mock meat and sesame oil contribute to total fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to half a dumpling, avoid additional sodium-containing condiments, and choose preparations with reduced soy sauce to keep sodium intake within daily cardiovascular targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 455 mg/100 g exceeds the RA threshold of 400 mg/100 g; glutinous rice is a very high-GI refined carbohydrate driving pro-inflammatory glycaemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should strictly limit portion size, request reduced soy sauce preparations, and pair with anti-inflammatory foods to mitigate the combined sodium and high-GI inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Rice Dumpling (素粽子, Su Zongzi) is a Singaporean Chinese festive food traditionally eaten during the Dragon Boat Festival (Duanwu). It consists of glutinous (sticky) rice packed with a savoury vegetarian filling of mock meat (textured vegetable protein), chestnut, and dried Chinese shiitake mushrooms, seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil, then wrapped tightly in dried bamboo leaves and steamed until fully cooked. The texture is dense, sticky, and chewy from the glutinous rice, with an earthy, umami-rich filling. The bamboo leaf imparts a subtle grassy fragrance. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 201 kcal, 5.9 g protein, 4.1 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate with 2.3 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 455 mg/100 g due to soy sauce seasoning. The glycaemic index of glutinous rice is approximately 98 (very high), making this food a significant concern for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The high sodium content also makes it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless portion size is strictly controlled. It is suitable for vegetarians but not certified halal or vegan. A typical serving is one dumpling (~180 g), delivering approximately 362 kcal and a glycaemic load of ~61.7.

Can diabetics eat vegetarian rice dumpling?

meal_context: festive snack during Dragon Boat Festival

confidence_reason: Core macros, secondary nutrients, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F07030013) with a stated confidence of 0.95. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for glutinous rice (GI ~98), which is the dominant ingredient. Fatty acid breakdown and several micronutrients are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1 accordingly.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this traditional steamed vegetarian rice dumpling are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F07030013) as of 2026-04-16, and no equivalent breakdown was found in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. GI of 98 applied based on glutinous rice analogue from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), Diabetes Care. GL per serving = 98 × (35.0 × 1.80) / 100 = 61.7 for a 180 g serving. Vegan flag set to false as mock meat products may contain egg-derived binders depending on brand; halal flag set to false as standard preparation uses soy sauce which may not be halal-certified. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
